whyExists: Different Python projects require different versions of external packages (e.g. Project A needs Django 3.2 while Project B needs Django 4.2). Installing packages globally creates version conflicts. A Virtual Environment (`venv`) is an isolated directory containing its own dedicated Python binary and `pip` package manager.
curiosityQuestion: What actually happens to your terminal prompt and environment when you run `source .venv/bin/activate`?
problemItSolves: Isolates project dependencies, preventing global package corruption and dependency conflicts.
realWorldAnalogy: A virtual environment is like a separate toolbox for each construction project: house building gets one toolbox, plumbing gets another. Tools don't mix or clutter each other.
simpleExplanation:
Commands:
1. Create: `python3 -m venv .venv`
2. Activate (macOS/Linux): `source .venv/bin/activate`
3. Activate (Windows): `.venv\Scripts\activate`
4. Deactivate: `deactivate`
5. Save dependencies: `pip freeze > requirements.txt`
syntaxExplanation:
# Terminal shell commands:
$ python3 -m venv .venv
$ source .venv/bin/activate
(.venv) $ pip install requests
(.venv) $ pip freeze > requirements.txt

examples.title: Venv Lifecycle Commands
examples.code:
# Create -> Activate -> Install -> Freeze -> Deactivate
$ python3 -m venv myenv
$ source myenv/bin/activate
(myenv) $ pip install flask
(myenv) $ deactivate
examples.explanation: Standard lifecycle workflow for project environments.

visualDiagram:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SystemPy["System Python /usr/bin/python"]
    ProjA["Project A (.venv1) -> Django 3.2"]
    ProjB["Project B (.venv2) -> Django 4.2"]
    SystemPy --> ProjA
    SystemPy --> ProjB
```

stepByStepExecution.explanation: Prepends .venv/bin directory to PATH environment variable.

memoryDiagram.stack: PATH env var updated to point to .venv/bin

namingRules: Name virtual environment directories `.venv` or `venv` and add to `.gitignore`!
commonMistakes: Committing `.venv` folder to Git repository! Add `.venv/` to `.gitignore`.
